title: Test Your Understanding: Communication Patterns
description: Test your knowledge of REST, gRPC, GraphQL, events, and the API gateway!

questions.question: What characterizes synchronous communication between services?

options.text: The caller emits an event and never waits
The caller sends a request and waits for the response
It always uses a message broker
It guarantees eventual consistency

questions.explanation: Synchronous means request/response: the caller blocks until it gets an answer. This couples the services in time, which is why a slow or down callee directly affects the caller.

questions.question: Why is REST often the default choice for public web APIs?

options.text: It uses a compact binary format
It's universal over HTTP, naturally cacheable, and simple to debug
It guarantees exactly-once delivery
It eliminates over-fetching

questions.explanation: REST rides on plain HTTP, so every client speaks it, GET requests cache well (including via CDNs), and you can test it with curl. Its weaknesses are chattiness and over/under-fetching.

questions.question: For what use case is gRPC the strongest fit?

options.text: A public API consumed directly by web browsers
Fast, strongly typed internal service-to-service communication
Caching static assets at the edge
Letting clients pick exactly which fields to return

questions.explanation: gRPC's compact binary format, strong .proto contracts, and HTTP/2 streaming make it excellent for internal services. It's awkward for browsers (needs a proxy) and harder to debug, so it's a poor public-facing choice.

questions.question: Which problems does GraphQL specifically address?

options.text: Over-fetching and under-fetching (too many round trips)
Message ordering and delivery guarantees
Single points of failure
Database sharding

questions.explanation: GraphQL lets the client request exactly the fields it needs in one query, avoiding downloading unused data (over-fetching) and multiple round trips (under-fetching). The trade-off is harder caching and query-cost control.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of event-driven communication between services?

options.text: It guarantees strong consistency
Loose coupling: the emitter doesn't know who reacts, so reactions can be added without changing it
It makes the call flow easier to trace
It removes the need for a database

questions.explanation: Emitting events decouples producers from consumers, so new reactions can subscribe without changing the producer. The trade-offs are harder tracing (no single call stack) and eventual consistency.

questions.question: A common recommendation is to start with a monolith and split out microservices only when a part has a real, separate scaling or ownership need.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Microservices impose a heavy distributed-systems tax (network calls, retries, deployment overhead). Starting monolithic keeps communication trivial; you split out services when there's a concrete reason.

questions.question: When you move from a monolith to microservices, what happens to a former in-process function call?

options.text: It stays exactly as fast and reliable
It becomes a network call subject to latency, failures, and serialization
It is automatically cached
It no longer needs error handling

questions.explanation: Across services, a call crosses the network, so it can be slow, fail, and needs serialization. This is the core tax of microservices and why resilience patterns become essential.

questions.question: Which responsibilities is an API gateway well-suited to handle? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Routing requests to the right backend service
Authentication and rate limiting at the edge
Aggregating multiple service calls into one client response
Owning the core business/domain logic of each service

questions.explanation: A gateway centralizes routing, auth, rate limiting, logging, and aggregation, giving clients one entry point. It should NOT become a home for business logic; the services own their domain logic.
